free_text: Absolute cross section of the 12C(p,gamma)13N reaction
Fachhochschule Bielefeld, 33619 Bielefeld,
Germany.
KN Van de Graaff accelerator at the
University of Notre Dame Nuclear Science Laboratory.
The Dynamitron Tandem Laboratory at
the University of Bochum.
A single, high resolution, 55% relative
efficiency, high purity germanium (HPGe) detector was
positioned at two distances from the target for
measurements in far (259 mm) and close (61 mm)
geometry between the detector and the water-cooled
target.
The isotopically enriched carbon targets were
produced by implantation at the Dynamitron Tandem
Laboratory at the University of Bochum by bombarding
0.5 mm backings of tungsten or tantalum with a 80
mu-A 12C beam at either 30 or 50 keV over an area
of 3.14 cm2. For the higher energy measurements at
the University of Notre Dame, an implantation energy
of 50 keV and a tungsten backing were used, while,
for the measurements at the Ep = 460 keV resonance
performed at the University of Bochum, an
implantation energy of 30 keV and tantalum were used.
Different implantation energies were used so that the
energy losses of the beam for the two measurements
were similar. For the 50 keV implantation energy
targets, a total of 0.73 C was deposited resulting in
an estimated 1.4*10**18 carbon atoms/cm2, while for
the 30 keV targets the charge deposited was 0.52 C
leading to 1.0*10**18 carbon atoms/cm2.
Total accumulated charge of 27 C, which was corrected
assuming a linear dependence of target thickness
deterioration with charge.
Approved by M.Wiescher and R.J.deBoer on
December 30, 2024.
BP
At the University of Notre Dame, an excitation curve
of the 12C(p,gamma)13N reaction was measured in
small, well calibrated energy steps between Ep = 1.0
and 2.5 MeV at 0 and 55. to expand the existing data
range of the reaction towards higher energies and to
study the interference patterns in this energy range.
From the present SRIM evaluation, the
estimated uncertainty in the stopping power is 5% over
the range of the present measurements. Therefore, a
total uncertainty in the 12C(p,gamma)13N cross section
of 6% is adopted.

10.1103/PhysRevC.108.035805 for the tabulated
experimental data and reaction rate.
In addition to the excitation curves, angular
distribution data were taken in 100 keV steps between
Ep = 1.2 and 2.49 MeV.
From the present SRIM evaluation, the
estimated uncertainty in the stopping power is 5%
over the range of the present measurements. Therefore,
a total uncertainty in the 12C(p,gamma) 13N cross
section of 6% is adopted.
See Supplemental Material at
http://link.aps.org/supplemental/
10.1103/PhysRevC.108.035805 for the tabulated
experimental data and reaction rate.
The activation runs were performed at five
energies between Ep = 1 and 2 MeV to obtain the
activation yields that have dominant components from
both the lowest energy Ep = 460 keV resonance and the
next highest at Ep = 1690 keV in the 12C(p,gamma)13N
reaction.
The uncertainty in the thick-target yield
measurements is 2.5%, with uncertainties coming from
the accuracy of the charge reading (2%), the gamma-
ray detection efficiency at 511 keV (1%, as
determined with a calibrated 22Na source), and
statistics (<1%).
